agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shipping, and completion."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing and creates a payment obligation. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and accepted into the system."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The point at which goods leave the warehouse for delivery to the customer."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy set by product management governing the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order in any pre-shipment state, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the order is cancelled successfully."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats this as a hard financial constraint — reversing an invoice has accounting, audit, and potentially legal implications. The system must not allow cancellation of invoiced orders.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order that has been invoiced, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. The timing could be changed by operations leadership.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a new order is placed, WHEN placement is confirmed, THEN an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed and is in a pre-shipment state","when":"A user requests cancellation of the order","then":"The system must determine whether the order is invoiced and apply the correct cancellation rule (this behavior is currently undefined due to contradictory requirements)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been placed","when":"The order placement is confirmed","then":"An invoice is generated immediately, locking in the order's pricing"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order has been invoiced","when":"A cancellation is requested","then":"The cancellation is rejected per Finance's constraint"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order exists but has NOT been invoiced","when":"A cancellation is requested before shipment","then":"The cancellation succeeds per Product's requirement"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (same transaction / milliseconds apart)","expected_behavior":"Undefined. If invoicing is synchronous at placement, the order is already invoiced and cannot be cancelled. If invoicing is async, there may be a race condition window where cancellation could succeed."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some line items shipped, others not","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The PM requirement says 'before it ships' but does not address partial shipments. The entire order is already invoiced at placement regardless."},{"scenario":"Invoice generation fails at placement","expected_behavior":"Undefined. If invoicing fails, is the order still placed? Can it then be cancelled since it technically is not invoiced?"},{"scenario":"Customer wants to cancel immediately after placing the order","expected_behavior":"This is the most common cancellation scenario in practice, yet under the current rules it is impossible — the order is already invoiced at placement."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by allowing cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance must implement credit notes or reversal processes, adding accounting complexity. If resolved by blocking all cancellations, customer satisfaction and refund policies are severely impacted.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution that decouples invoice timing from placement requires Operations to find an alternative mechanism for price-locking (e.g., price snapshots without formal invoicing).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders shortly after placement (industry standard). If cancellation is impossible from the moment of placement, customer experience degrades significantly, likely increasing support volume and churn.","risk":"High risk of shipping the wrong behavior. If developers pick one stakeholder's rule over another without resolution, either financial integrity (cancelled invoices) or customer experience (no cancellations ever) is compromised.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order under current rules, since every order is invoiced at placement. This contradicts standard e-commerce expectations."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is permitted, they need credit note / invoice reversal workflows, impacting reconciliation and audit trails."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoice timing is moved to pre-shipment instead of placement, they lose their price-locking mechanism and need an alternative."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is effectively impossible."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Their stated cancellation policy cannot be fulfilled under the current invoicing rules."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other, making it impossible to implement a correct system without resolution. BR-1 + BR-3 combined make BR-2 a total cancellation blocker, yet BR-1 explicitly promises cancellations are allowed. The spec quality is low because no stakeholder accounted for the others' constraints.","high_confidence_areas":["Each individual requirement is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The contradiction is deterministic and provable: invoiced-at-placement + no-cancel-if-invoiced = no cancellations ever, which violates the PM requirement"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note mechanism as distinct from 'cancelling an invoice'","Whether Operations could accept a price-lock mechanism that is not a formal invoice","Whether there are additional order states between placement and shipment that might affect the analysis"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous — the order is invoiced before any cancellation window opens","'Ships' means the physical act of dispatching goods, not a status change","There are no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal processes in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy when combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) and BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled). The transitive conclusion is that NO order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting BR-1. Three stakeholders have provided mutually exclusive requirements.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Decouple price-locking from invoicing — Operations uses a price snapshot at placement, invoice is generated later (e.g., at shipment). Cancellation is allowed before invoicing. Preserves PM and Finance rules, requires Operations to change process.","B: Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes — Finance introduces a reversal mechanism. Invoice itself is not 'cancelled' but offset. Preserves PM and Operations rules, requires Finance to accept credit-note workflows.","C: Restrict cancellation to a short grace period before invoicing — introduce a brief async delay between placement and invoicing. Preserves Finance and Operations intent, but limits PM's 'any time before shipment' to a narrow window.","D: Accept that cancellation is not possible post-placement and update the PM requirement accordingly. Preserves Finance and Operations rules, but fundamentally changes the product offering."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the industry-standard resolution. Most e-commerce and logistics systems treat invoice reversal via credit notes as a normal financial operation. This preserves the customer-facing cancellation policy, maintains Operations' price-locking at placement, and gives Finance an auditable reversal trail without literally 'cancelling' an invoice."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Any implementation that claims to satisfy all three is logically incorrect.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3."}]}

Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shippin

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these three requirements:

1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

This is a clear contradiction:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled.
- But the PM says orders can be cancelled any time before shipping.

These are explicit, directly conflicting requirements from different stakeholders. 

So I'm looking at a fundamental contradiction between what these three teams are asking for.

If developers pick one stakeholder's rule over another without resolution, either financial integrity (cancelled invoices) or customer experience (no cancellations ever) is compromised.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order under current rules, since every order is invoiced at placement. This contradicts standard e-commerce expectations."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is permitted, they need credit note / invoice reversal workflows, impacting reconciliation and audit trails."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoice timing is moved to pre-shipment instead of placement, they lose their price-locking mechanism and need an alternative."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is effectively impossible."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Their stated cancellation policy cannot be fulfilled under the current invoicing rules."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other, making it impossible to implement a correct system without resolution. The spec quality is low because no stakeholder accounted for the others' constraints.","high_confidence_areas":["Each individual requirement is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The contradiction is deterministic and provable: invoiced-at-placement + no-cancel-if-invoiced = no cancellations ever, which violates the PM requirement"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note mechanism as distinct from 'cancelling an invoice'","Whether Operations could accept a price-lock mechanism that is not a formal invoice","Whether there are additional order states between placement and shipment that might affect the analysis"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous — the order is invoiced before any cancellation window opens","'Ships' means the physical act of dispatching goods, not a status change","There are no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal processes in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy when combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) and BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled). The transitive conclusion is that NO order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting BR-1. Three stakeholders have provided mutually exclusive requirements.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Decouple price-locking from invoicing — Operations uses a price snapshot at placement, invoice is generated later (e.g., at shipment). Cancellation is allowed before invoicing. Preserves PM and Finance rules, requires Operations to change process.","B: Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes — Finance introduces a reversal mechanism. Invoice itself is not 'cancelled' but offset. Preserves PM and Operations rules, requires Finance to accept credit-note workflows.","C: Restrict cancellation to a short grace period before invoicing — introduce a brief async delay between placement and invoicing. Preserves Finance and Operations intent, but limits PM's 'any time before shipment' to a narrow window.","D: Accept that cancellation is not possible post-placement and update the PM requirement accordingly. Preserves Finance and Operations rules, but fundamentally changes the product offering."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the industry-standard resolution. Most e-commerce and logistics systems treat invoice reversal via credit notes as a normal financial operation. This preserves the customer-facing cancellation policy, maintains Operations' price-locking at placement, and gives Finance an auditable reversal trail without literally 'cancelling' an invoice."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Any implementation that claims to satisfy all three is logically incorrect.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3."}]}
